source_paper_title: Minimization Fractional Prophet Inequalities for Sequential Procurement

Page 6, end of Section 1.3 (Our results) and reiterated on page 18, end of Section 4.2 (More than two sellers)

This helps give some intuition as to why computing the precise competitive ratio for all \(n \ge 2, p > 1\) is a challenging task. We leave it as an open question to analytically resolve the competitive ratio.

Computing the exact competitive ratio as a function of the problem parameters \((p, n, \ell, u)\) is a challenging problem.
title: Compute competitive ratio for fractional minimization prophet inequalities without in-house production
background_latex:
Fix integers \(n\ge 2\) and a real exponent \(p>1\). Let \(\ell,u\) satisfy \(0<\ell\le u<\infty\). For each stage \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\), a cost coefficient \(C_i\) is drawn independently from an (a priori known) distribution \(D_i\) supported on \([\ell,u]\). After observing \(C_i=c_i\), an online algorithm chooses a purchase amount \(x_i\in[0,1]\), where \(x_i\) may depend on \((c_1,\dots,c_i)\), and must satisfy the ex post feasibility constraint \(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i = 1\). The realized procurement cost under exponent \(p\) is
\[
\mathrm{ALG}_\pi(c,p)=\sum_{i=1}^n c_i x_i^p,
\]
where \(\pi=(\pi_1,\dots,\pi_n)\) denotes the online policy and \(x_i=\pi_i(c_1,\dots,c_i)\).

For a realized vector \(c=(c_1,\dots,c_n)\), define the clairvoyant (prophet) benchmark as the optimal value of the offline convex program
\[
\mathrm{OPT}(c,p)=\min_{x\in[0,1]^n}\ \sum_{i=1}^n c_i x_i^p\quad\text{s.t.}\quad \sum_{i=1}^n x_i=1.
\]
The competitive ratio for the family \(\mathcal D\) of allowable distributions is
\[
\mathrm{CR}(\mathcal D,p,n)=\inf_{\pi}\ \sup_{(D_1,\dots,D_n)\in\mathcal D^n}\ \frac{\mathbb E[\mathrm{ALG}_\pi(C,p)]}{\mathbb E[\mathrm{OPT}(C,p)]},
\]
where the expectation is over \(C_i\sim D_i\) independently.

In the "without in-house production" setting, there is no additional deterministic option \(C_1\equiv 1\); all \(n\) stages correspond to random sellers, and the supremum ranges over \(D_1,\dots,D_n\) in the allowed class (e.g., supported on \([\ell,u]\)).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine (in closed form, as a function of \(p,n,\ell,u\)) the competitive ratio
\[
\mathrm{CR}(\mathcal D,p,n)=\inf_{\pi}\ \sup_{D_1,\dots,D_n\ \text{supported on }[\ell,u]}\ \frac{\mathbb E[\mathrm{ALG}_\pi(C,p)]}{\mathbb E[\mathrm{OPT}(C,p)]}
\]
for the fractional minimization prophet inequality without in-house production (i.e., with \(n\) random sellers only), beyond the special case \(p=2,n=2\) treated explicitly in the paper.

location_in_paper: Page 6, end of Section 1.3 (Our results) and reiterated on page 18, end of Section 4.2 (More than two sellers)
open_problem_quote:
This helps give some intuition as to why computing the precise competitive ratio for all \(n \ge 2, p > 1\) is a challenging task. We leave it as an open question to analytically resolve the competitive ratio.

Computing the exact competitive ratio as a function of the problem parameters \((p, n, \ell, u)\) is a challenging problem.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: For fixed \((p,n,\ell,u)\) and a discretized support, the worst-case distribution search becomes a large but finitely parameterized nonconvex optimization that can be brute-forced or locally optimized, potentially revealing structure or candidate extremal distributions. However, the true problem is infinite-dimensional over distributions, so computational searches can suggest conjectures but not decisively refute/confirm the exact formula without additional theory.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate policies \(\pi\) (e.g., parameterized thresholding/dynamic-programming approximations) and candidate hard instances (parameterized discrete distributions on \([\ell,u]\)) can both be represented in code, with an automatic fitness signal given by Monte Carlo estimation of \(\mathbb E[\mathrm{ALG}_\pi]/\mathbb E[\mathrm{OPT}]\) and inner optimization over \(x\) for \(\mathrm{OPT}\). An evolutionary loop could search jointly over policy families and adversarial distributions to conjecture sharp bounds or identify extremal distribution structures for given \((p,n,\ell,u)\).

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper formulates a fractional minimization prophet inequality for sequential procurement with monomial costs \(\sum_i c_i x_i^p\) and ex post constraint \(\sum_i x_i=1\). Offline, the convex program has a closed form \(\mathrm{OPT}(c,p)=(\sum_i c_i^q)^{1/q}\) with \(q=-1/(p-1)\), i.e., a negative power mean of the realized coefficients. Online, the paper resolves two regimes: for “well-behaved” bounded distributions (invertible CDF with Lipschitz inverse), a multi-threshold policy achieves \(\mathrm{CR}=1+O(1/\log n)\); and with an in-house deterministic option, the worst-case competitive ratio over supports \([\ell,\infty)\) is exactly \((1+(n-1)\ell^{-1/(p-1)})^{p-1}\), driven by long-shot distributions.

Without in-house production, the paper fully solves only the special case \(p=2,n=2\), obtaining \(\mathrm{CR}=\tfrac14(\sqrt{u/\ell}+\sqrt{\ell/u})+\tfrac12\) via an explicit extremal distribution analysis under a mean constraint and harmonic-mean structure. For \(p=2\) it also derives a dynamic-program recursion for the optimal policy in terms of iterated harmonic means, and shows that unlike the classical (maximization, stopping) prophet inequality, the worst case does not reduce to \(n=2\) and the competitive ratio can be non-monotone in \(n\). These phenomena indicate that any closed-form \(\mathrm{CR}(\mathcal D,p,n)\) for bounded supports \([\ell,u]\) will likely require new extremal arguments that handle both (i) the dependence of \(\mathbb E[\mathrm{OPT}(C,p)]\) on a negative power mean and (ii) the richer feasible set of fractional allocations.

Beyond the source paper, the closest prior work on minimization prophet-type problems includes Prophet Secretary and hiring/coverage-style minimization prophet models. While these do not directly address convex fractional procurement, they highlight that minimization objectives often exhibit unboundedness without bounded supports and that feasibility constraints beyond stopping can fundamentally change extremal instances. Promising directions suggested by the source paper’s numerics are to restrict attention to extremal distributions (point masses and long-shot/two-point laws) and to search for structural monotonicity/threshold properties in the worst-case sequence \(D_1,\dots,D_n\) (e.g., early point masses followed by increasingly long-shot distributions), potentially enabling an analytic characterization for general \(n\) at least when \(p=2\), and then extending to general \(p>1\) using the negative power-mean form of \(\mathrm{OPT}(c,p)\).

sources.title: Minimization Fractional Prophet Inequalities for Sequential Procurement
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces fractional minimization prophet inequalities with monomial costs \(\sum_i c_i x_i^p\) under \(\sum_i x_i=1\). Gives asymptotically optimal multi-threshold policy for well-behaved bounded distributions, exact competitive ratio with in-house production for arbitrary \(p>1\), and exact competitive ratio without in-house production only for \(p=2,n=2\): \(\tfrac14(\sqrt{u/\ell}+\sqrt{\ell/u})+\tfrac12\). Shows \(n=2\) is not worst-case for \(p=2\) and competitive ratio can be non-monotone in \(n\).
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the open problem: determine \(\mathrm{CR}(\mathcal D,p,n)\) without in-house production for general \(p>1\), \(n\ge2\), distributions supported on \([\ell,u]\). Provides key structural lemmas: offline optimum equals \((\sum_i c_i^q)^{1/q}\) with \(q=-1/(p-1)\), and for \(p=2\) the optimal online policy has a harmonic-mean recursion.

sources.title: Prophet secretary
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies minimization-type prophet/secretary variants; establishes strong lower bounds for minimization prophet settings (including unboundedness when costs can be arbitrarily large and exponential dependence on \(n\) in certain models).
sources.connection: Provides baseline impossibility phenomena for minimization prophet inequalities (especially when \(u\to\infty\) or \(p=1\)), motivating why bounded support \([\ell,u]\) and \(p>1\) are essential in the open problem.

sources.title: Hiring secretaries over time: The benefit of concurrent employment
sources.theorem_or_result: Minimization prophet-style online decision problem with overlapping commitments; develops competitive algorithms and bounds in a setting that is not a pure stopping problem.
sources.connection: Closest minimization-prophet literature beyond the source paper; offers techniques for minimization objectives with feasibility constraints that are not simple stopping, potentially inspirational for extending beyond \(p=2,n=2\) without in-house production.

sources.title: A survey of prophet inequalities in optimal stopping theory
sources.theorem_or_result: Survey of classical (maximization, stopping-rule) prophet inequalities, including extremal distribution constructions and the fact that worst-case ratios often reduce to \(n=2\) in classical settings.
sources.connection: The source paper contrasts its setting with classical results (e.g., worst case not attained at \(n=2\)); this survey provides the classical extremal-method context that may guide attempts to characterize worst-case distributions for the fractional convex minimization setting.

sources.title: Ratio comparisons of supremum and stop rule expectations
sources.theorem_or_result: In classical prophet inequalities, shows worst-case competitive ratio can be realized with \(n=2\) and characterizes extremal distributions for that case.
sources.connection: Serves as a methodological foil: the open problem asks for closed-form \(\mathrm{CR}(\mathcal D,p,n)\) without in-house production, but the source paper shows the classical reduction-to-\(n=2\) fails for \(p=2\). Understanding why may require adapting (or proving impossibility of adapting) Hill–Kertz extremal arguments to convex fractional costs.
